summary: McKenzie responds to Seth Godin's commentary on Starbucks' failed email coupon promotion that went viral beyond its intended audience. He proposes a lightweight solution: ask customers for their first name and last four phone digits before honoring the coupon, creating just enough friction to deter mass abuse without actually building a verification system. He argues the real business calculus favors honoring the drinks since the marginal cost is trivial compared to brand damage from visibly canceling a promotion. The post demonstrates his characteristic focus on understanding the actual economics of a business decision rather than the surface-level problem.
key_points: Starbucks created a viral coupon problem by designing an email promotion as a chain letter with no controls on distribution
The optimal response is minimal friction that keeps honest people honest, not a real verification system — the appearance of tracking is sufficient deterrence
The marginal cost of honoring abused coupons is trivial (drinks cost ~10 cents to make) compared to the brand damage of publicly canceling a promotion
Security theater has legitimate business applications when the cost of actual enforcement exceeds the cost of the fraud
money_quotes: The only purpose of this system is to keep honest men honest and to remind folks that there is no presumptive right to free Starbucks.
Here's the secret to Starbucks: no matter how much they charge you, making the drink didn't cost more than 10 cents!
The damage to the brand from having to put that sign up everywhere, on the other hand, is at literally many orders of magnitude above the individual store.
The impact of one scammer who realizes he can beat the system (and, if you think of it, there is no system to beat here) is one ice drink per store.
That plus the fact that it is recorded is just enough to make them remember 'Oh, thats right, I'm being watched'.
key_insight: When the cost of fraud is trivially small per incident but the brand damage of visibly fighting it is enormous, the right move is to honor the promotion with just enough friction to discourage casual abuse.
